external.description:
Instead of starting your work in a blank Word document or spreadsheet, start in Copilot chat, then move it to a Copilot page, which is a Loop file. From there, you drop part of the page, a component, into chat for live collaboration with your team, or you work together in the Loop app, with Copilot assisting again and drop the new draft into an email, linked to the web version for easier editing by the entire team, until finally, at the very end of this process, you create a PDF of the final product, and add it to your "records" location. 

Thus, only the final version ever gets stored as a record. All the prior versions are gone. Work that is still in progress is not a record, but accessible to AI tools. This is the conundrum for RIM.
external.title: Worth Reading – RIM Is Moving Upstream

record.text: Copilot のレビューが段々良い感じになってきている気がする。元から速かったのに加えて、CodexとかClaudeよりも指摘の数自体は出してくれる。表層的な指摘が多そうな気はするが、Claudeに判断させたらほとんどが妥当な指摘らしい。CodeRabbitはもう解約してしまったが、CodeRabbit との比較ではどんなもんなのだろう。

Tursoだとプルリクごとのプレビュー環境を作って壊すのが気軽にできる。Claudeにちょっと気になったこととか外出先からスマホで指示出して、プルリクできたらプレビュー環境にスマホでアクセスして動作確認してダメ出しし、コードレビューはCopilotにやらせている。居酒屋で酒飲みながら開発できる。自前のタスク管理ツールをWebツールとしてホストしてるから次に何やらせれば良いかもスマホからわかる。タスクのソースはリポジトリにマークダウンファイルとして置いてるからAIも普通に読める。ただタスク管理はGitHub Issuesに回帰しても良いかなと考え中
コード書く道具としてのAIには正しいことだけ言って欲しいけど、チャットの相手としてはハルシネーションたくさんするくらいの方がちょうどいい。適当な話には適当に返して欲しい

I built Earshot.

Agents listen to audio (a mic and my ham radio), transcribe it, and write it to shared memory. A chatbot reads it and chats about it.

The interesting bit, the agents never talk directly. They just share memory. Agent coordination is just decoupling.

金融时报：美国的“另一部分”经济，讲述了一个截然不同的增长故事

到处都在说增长很难。经济不是零和游戏，但比几年前更接近零和游戏。

美国很多人所在的行业，看起来更像消费品行业，而不是科技行业。这解释了美国经济中许多表面上的错位：低失业率与糟糕消费者信心之间的错位，强劲国内生产总值增长与行业调查之间的错位。

由投资驱动、情绪高涨的科技经济，与由消费驱动、竞争残酷的“另一种”经济之间，距离正在扩大。两边都要关注。

record.text:
从5月来看，申请失业救济人数仍处于低位，显示周五即将公布的就业报告中，失业率可能再次稳定在4.3%。

不过，第一季度劳动生产率比最初公布的数据还要疲软。

美国劳工统计局周四公布的数据显示，第一季度非农商业部门劳动生产率按年率计算增长0.3%。这比劳工统计局最初公布的0.8%疲软增速还要大幅下修。

增长乏力的原因是，今年前三个月的产出增幅从1.5%下修至仅1%。这个季度的工作时长增幅仍为0.7%。

随着人工智能的采用，劳动生产率受到关注。它衡量的是劳动者生产商品和服务的速度与效率。劳工统计局每季度计算生产率，方法是用实际产出除以总工作时长。这组数据通常波动较大，大幅修正也很常见。（巴伦）

截至5月底，美国申请失业救济的人数意外偏高，但仍低于令人担忧的水平，但修正后的劳动生产率数据疲软。

截至5月30日当周，首次申请失业救济人数小幅上升至22.5万人，比前一周修正后的21.2万人增加1.3万人，略高于经济学家对月底数据的预期，但仍显示全美裁员水平相对较低。

作为统计参考的这一周包含阵亡将士纪念日假期，这很可能给这一组数据带来一些波动，幅度甚至可能超过通常的周度起伏。

剔除部分波动后的四周移动平均值为21.475万人。这个数字略高于今年以来的平均水平，但仍明显低于2月的水平。

record.text: This is technically feasible. When air quality is poor in one region, compute jobs can shift. When a power grid runs clean, that is when the heaviest processing runs.

The researchers do not merely diagnose. They propose a path. They describe a "health-informed computing framework" — a methodology by which data center workloads can be scheduled across time and geography to minimize human harm.

The companies gaining trillions in market value from artificial intelligence have not yet been asked to account for the bodies. This paper is the beginning of that accounting.

What Must Be Done

Where Kuroda Goes Beyond Kant

Kuroda’s framework appears to diverge from Kant in a crucial respect.
For Kant, the structures of cognition are universal and fixed.
The categories of understanding provide the stable framework within which experience occurs.

The difference lies in tone.
Erasmus uses irony and humor.
Kant uses transcendental analysis.
Yet both dismantle false certainty.

The Critique of Pure Reason could almost be described as a philosophical counterpart to The Praise of Folly.
Both works reveal that human reason tends to overestimate itself.

record.text: When other businesses get between us and our readers, we become vulnerable to their decisions.

external.description:
If you’ve been reading me for any length of time, you’ll know I’m obsessed with publishers, publications, and journalists owning their relationships with audiences, not “borrowing” them from platforms and other businesses. I usually frame that in terms of the benefits – but how about the threats of not doing it?

Right now, there are two swords of Damocles dangling over publishers – one physical, one digital; one UK-based, one global – that could destroy titles and businesses that haven’t truly absorbed this idea. And the publishers have no impact on the chances of that sword falling.

Let’s go physical and local first.

## UK Magazines after TG Jones

This first came to my attention because, of all things, a recommended post on Facebook. This may be the first time the Facebook algorithm has delivered me something unexpected and useful. It was from a group (which I’m not a member of) called The Mighty World of British Comics, and it seems to be where aging fans and creators of UK comics from the _Beano_ to things like _2000AD_ hang out.

And they’re worried:

> Does anyone realise that if TG Jones, goes under in the UK.........magazines and comics, will collapse? They are the only UK retailer for mags and comics left in the UK.

For non-UK readers, TG Jones is the new name for the high street branches of the venerable newsagents WH Smiths. A couple of years ago, the original company sold off everything bar the transport hub shops, and the new venture capital owners rebranded it to the mind-numbingly generic TG Jones.

TG Jones in Worthing and Shoreham-by-Sea

### Magazine discovery under threat

That acquisition hasn’t gone well:

> TG Jones will likely go into administration at the end of June if a wide-scale restructuring plan, involving the restructuring of its financial, leasehold and supply obligations, is not legally sanctioned, The Bookseller understands.

This could be a serious blow for the UK magazine industry. Supermarkets have been reducing their selection of mags for years.

The limited selection of magazines in my local supermarket

The number of newsagents that still have a significant range of titles is vanishingly small — most are basically convenience stores these days. The major discovery channel for UK magazines might be about to implode. If the company does go down – or even if they drastically decrease their number of shops – publishers face both short-term hits from the loss of sales, and longer-term declines as discovery erodes.

Magazines can survive and throve as subscription businesses, but that’s only going to get harder if their major discovery channel all but disappears from physical shops. Frankly, many magazine publishers need much, much better digital presences than they do now, if they want to survive a TG Jones collapse.

And they may only have a month.

## Google breaks its contract with the web

“Google Zero” is the latest panic gripping the publishing industry, in its endless oscillation from sticking its head in the sand over new tech, and then completely over-reacting when it does pay attention. It’s the idea that, as Google gets ever more focused on AI as the core mode of its search engine, traffic to websites will crash. I’m skeptical that things will be that extreme, but let’s assume, for now, that this is a major risk.

In that case, if you still reply on SEO as a major component of your discovery, you might need a change of underwear:

> The era of the “ten blue links” is officially over.

See? I’ll wait while you change.

OK, here’s some detail:

> Instead of returning a simple list of links, Google Search will drop users into AI-powered interactive experiences at times. Google is also introducing tools that can dispatch “information agents” to gather information on a user’s behalf, along with tools that let users build personalized mini apps tailored to their needs.

Here’s a Google video outlining the change:

Now, look, the smart money has already been reducing reliance on SEO as a major audience acquisition vector, let alone a key monetisation approach. But this is only going to increase the downwards pressure on traffic from search.

## Publishers need to own their audience relationships

We’re always going to use other channels – ones we don’t own – as part of the discovery mechanism of our publications. But we need to be incredibly careful when one vector becomes dominant. The slow collapse of the UK newstrade has left TG Jones on outsized player – and this is a threat. And the monopolistic power of Google over search has made it equally dangerous.

Where are the vulnerabilities in your audience acquisition model?
external.title: Two examples why publishers need to own their audience relationships

It’s the idea that, as Google gets ever more focused on AI as the core mode of its search engine, traffic to websites will crash. I’m skeptical that things will be that extreme, but let’s assume, for now, that this is a major risk.

In that case, if you still reply on SEO as a major component of your discovery, you might need a change of underwear:

The era of the “ten blue links” is officially over.

See? I’ll wait while you change.

OK, here’s some detail:

Instead of returning a simple list of links, Google Search will drop users into AI-powered interactive experiences at times. Google is also introducing tools that can dispatch “information agents” to gather information on a user’s behalf, along with tools that let users build personalized mini apps tailored to their needs.

Here’s a Google video outlining the change:

Now, look, the smart money has already been reducing reliance on SEO as a major audience acquisition vector, let alone a key monetisation approach. But this is only going to increase the downwards pressure on traffic from search.

Publishers need to own their audience relationships

We’re always going to use other channels – ones we don’t own – as part of the discovery mechanism of our publications. But we need to be incredibly careful when one vector becomes dominant. The slow collapse of the UK newstrade has left TG Jones on outsized player – and this is a threat. And the monopolistic power of Google over search has made it equally dangerous.

Where are the vulnerabilities in your audience acquisition model?

external.description:
If you’ve been reading me for any length of time, you’ll know I’m obsessed with publishers, publications, and journalists owning their relationships with audiences, not “borrowing” them from platforms and other businesses. I usually frame that in terms of the benefits – but how about the threats of not doing it?

Right now, there are two swords of Damocles dangling over publishers – one physical, one digital; one UK-based, one global – that could destroy titles and businesses that haven’t truly absorbed this idea. And the publishers have no impact on the chances of that sword falling.

Let’s go physical and local first.

## UK Magazines after TG Jones

This first came to my attention because, of all things, a recommended post on Facebook. This may be the first time the Facebook algorithm has delivered me something unexpected and useful. It was from a group (which I’m not a member of) called The Mighty World of British Comics, and it seems to be where aging fans and creators of UK comics from the _Beano_ to things like _2000AD_ hang out.

And they’re worried:

> Does anyone realise that if TG Jones, goes under in the UK.........magazines and comics, will collapse? They are the only UK retailer for mags and comics left in the UK.

For non-UK readers, TG Jones is the new name for the high street branches of the venerable newsagents WH Smiths. A couple of years ago, the original company sold off everything bar the transport hub shops, and the new venture capital owners rebranded it to the mind-numbingly generic TG Jones.

TG Jones in Worthing and Shoreham-by-Sea

### Magazine discovery under threat

That acquisition hasn’t gone well:

> TG Jones will likely go into administration at the end of June if a wide-scale restructuring plan, involving the restructuring of its financial, leasehold and supply obligations, is not legally sanctioned, The Bookseller understands.

This could be a serious blow for the UK magazine industry. Supermarkets have been reducing their selection of mags for years.

The limited selection of magazines in my local supermarket

The number of newsagents that still have a significant range of titles is vanishingly small — most are basically convenience stores these days. The major discovery channel for UK magazines might be about to implode. If the company does go down – or even if they drastically decrease their number of shops – publishers face both short-term hits from the loss of sales, and longer-term declines as discovery erodes.

Magazines can survive and throve as subscription businesses, but that’s only going to get harder if their major discovery channel all but disappears from physical shops. Frankly, many magazine publishers need much, much better digital presences than they do now, if they want to survive a TG Jones collapse.

And they may only have a month.

record.text: A living list of the reasons I think the panic is over-blown - and any evidence I can gather

external.description:
One of the current bogeymen of publishers is what's variously called “zero click search” or “Google zero”. It's the idea that, as Google introduces more and more Ai into their search engine, traffic from search will trend to zero – or maybe even disappear altogether.

Right now, I'm of the opinion that this is overblown. I think traffic from search _will_ drop, but different types of content will be more impacted than others. But I don't think the fundamental "search for something to read/watch/listen" element will ever entirely disappear.

But for future linking purposes, I've decided to create this little living article where I can add thoughts and links to support my skepticism – or acknowledge that I was wrong, if we do end up in a zero click world.

## Why am I skeptical?

  * Google has said that the links are not going away. In essence, you’ll get different search experiences based on your search intent.
  * The whole business model for AI (such as it is) collapses if there isn’t a steady supply of fresh material to train on. Disincentivising the creation of new original content is literally starving the AI businesses.
  * There’s consumer resistance to AI-driven search, with other search engines like DuckDuckGo benefiting, as people shift to AI-light or AI-free alternatives.
  * Fundamentally, zero click search assumed that the _only_ reason people use search is for commodity information – and that's not borne out by the reality of search patterns.
external.title: Reasons to be skeptical about (total) Zero Click Search

record.text: Quality context is the hard part – their examples show agents failing when they can't distinguish between a minor bug report and a critical outage. Without that human curation layer, you end up with very confident AI making very wrong decisions.

Their "company brain" still needs humans to teach it what matters – the real challenge is getting teams to actually feed it quality context consistently.

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48387095

The compute overhead they mention for constitutional AI is substantial – running multiple inference passes per query. That's why we see such a gap between base model benchmarks and what ships in production.

record.text:
SOC 2 Type II = the vendor has baseline security controls.

It does NOT mean their audit trail is tamper-evident, or that a model decision can be reconstructed at time T.

"Audited vendor" and "audit-ready output" are different claims. In regulated AI, only the second passes a review.

RAG beats fine-tuning in regulated environments — not on accuracy, on audit trail.

Fine-tune lineage is baked into weights: probabilistic, hard to audit. RAG lineage is explicit: these docs, this timestamp. Deterministic, verifiable.

That's why regulated AI goes retrieval-first.

Why Rust for an LLM gateway: a BYOK proxy forwards thousands of requests/sec on the hot path. Python's GIL ceiling becomes the cost of staying in Python.

Rust keeps the hot path tight — async without boxing, monomorphized dispatch. The gateway is where every request lives or dies.

We turn your LinkedIn profile into a blockbuster trailer starring ...you. 🎬

A few weeks ago, this was out of reach. Today, it’s real.

The wall was continuity: faces would shapeshift, backgrounds reconfigured, physics got floaty, camera language glitched, and even text turned into hieroglyphs. You could get impressive fragments, but stitching them into a real trailer made everything fall apart.

Then the new wave of image + video models unlocked reference-first workflows: generate stable character images, then animate from those references instead of reinventing the person every clip.

At a hackathon we tried again. We treated it like a film set with a brilliant but very confused crew, and gave it fixed shot lists, strict timing, and repeatable camera instructions etc.

The first stitched trailer was a banger. Same face across clips. Cinematic movement. Pacing that actually breathes.

People loved it, so we turned our prototype into a production platform: dicapri.you
